title: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce

seo.title: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole) - Easy Steps
seo.metaDescription: Master authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) with our easy recipe! Dive into the rich, complex flavors of this traditional Mexican chicken dish. Perfect for any meal.
seo.h1: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole)
h2Headings: What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?
Step-by-Step Instructions for Pollo en Mole
Tips for the Perfect Chicken Mole Sauce
Variations and Substitutions for Mole Sauce
seo.imageAlt: A plate of authentic Mexican Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) with chicken pieces, rich dark sauce, sesame seeds, and rice.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the deep, complex flavors of traditional Mexican Pollo en Mole, a truly iconic and satisfying chicken dish.

description: Pollo en Mole is a quintessential Mexican dish, featuring tender chicken bathed in a rich, complex sauce made from chiles, spices, nuts, and a hint of chocolate. This traditional recipe delivers a symphony of flavors, balancing sweet, savory, and subtly spicy notes. It is a true celebration of Mexican culinary heritage, perfect for special occasions or a comforting family meal.

ingredients.notes: about 1.5 oz, or 1 oz unsweetened chocolate

instructions.title: Prepare the Chicken
instructions.text: In a large pot, simmer chicken pieces in 4 cups of water with a pinch of salt until cooked through, about 25-30 minutes. Remove chicken, reserving 2 cups of the cooking liquid. Set chicken aside.

instructions.title: Prepare the Chiles
instructions.text: Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat. Toast the dried chiles briefly, about 30 seconds per side, until fragrant, being careful not to burn them. Immediately transfer to a bowl and cover with hot water to rehydrate for 15-20 minutes until soft. Drain and reserve.

instructions.title: Toast Nuts and Spices
instructions.text: In the same dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds, and raisins separately until lightly golden and fragrant. Set aside. Lightly toast the onion, garlic, tomato, and stale tortilla/bread until slightly charred.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Base
instructions.text: In a blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, toasted nuts, seeds, raisins, onion, garlic, tomato, tortilla/bread, cinnamon, cloves, anise, black pepper, and oregano. Add 1 cup of the reserved chicken cooking liquid or chicken broth. Blend until very smooth, adding more liquid if needed to create a thick paste.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot oil (it will splatter). Cook, stirring constantly, for about 10-15 minutes until the sauce darkens and thickens significantly, reducing in volume. This step is crucial for developing flavor.

instructions.title: Simmer and Season
instructions.text: Gradually stir in the remaining 1 cup of reserved chicken cooking liquid or chicken broth, along with the Mexican chocolate. Bring to a simmer, then reduce heat to low. Cook, stirring occasionally, for at least 20-30 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld and the sauce to thicken to your desired consistency. Season with salt and a pinch of sugar if needed to balance the flavors.

instructions.title: Combine and Serve
instructions.text: Add the cooked chicken pieces to the mole sauce and gently coat them. Simmer for another 5-10 minutes to heat the chicken through and allow it to absorb the mole flavors. Serve hot, garnished with extra sesame seeds and accompanied by rice and warm tortillas.

tips: For a deeper, richer mole, pan-fry the chicken pieces after boiling until golden brown before adding them to the sauce.
If you cannot find specific dried chiles, a good quality mole paste can be used as a base, then enhanced with fresh aromatics and chocolate.
Leftover mole sauce freezes beautifully for up to 3 months. Thaw and reheat gently, adding a splash of broth if it's too thick.
article:
Discovering the heart of Mexican cuisine often leads one down a path of vibrant flavors, rich histories, and culinary traditions passed down through generations. Among these gastronomic treasures, few dishes command as much respect and adoration as Pollo en Mole, or Chicken Mole. This isn't just a meal; it's an experience, a symphony of ingredients harmonizing to create a sauce so complex and profound it's almost legendary. If you've ever been intimidated by the thought of making authentic Chicken Mole, fear not. This guide will demystify the process, inviting you into the wonderful world of Mexican chicken recipes and empowering you to create a truly unforgettable dish in your own kitchen.

## What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?

At its core, "mole" (pronounced MOH-lay) refers to a category of rich, intricate sauces in Mexican cuisine, rather than a single dish. There are dozens, if not hundreds, of mole variations across Mexico, each with its own unique regional identity and ingredient profile. However, when most people think of mole, they're likely imagining Mole Poblano, the most famous and arguably the most complex of them all, originating from Puebla. This is the sauce most commonly associated with Chicken Mole.

A true mole sauce is a testament to culinary patience and skill, often containing a staggering list of 20 to 30 or even more ingredients. These typically include various dried chiles (ancho, guajillo, pasilla, mulato), nuts and seeds (almonds, peanuts, sesame, pumpkin seeds), spices (cumin, coriander, cinnamon, cloves, anise), aromatics (onion, garlic, tomato, tomatillo), and, famously, a touch of chocolate. The chocolate, often Mexican table chocolate, isn't there to make the sauce sweet, but rather to add depth, balance the heat from the chiles, and contribute to its characteristic dark, glossy finish. The resulting sauce is a masterful blend of sweet, savory, spicy, earthy, and sometimes smoky notes, creating an unparalleled depth of flavor that makes Chicken Mole a truly extraordinary dish.

## Ingredients for Authentic Chicken Mole

Crafting an authentic mole sauce from scratch is a labor of love, but the reward is immense. Here’s a breakdown of the key components you’ll need for a classic Chicken Mole:

For the Chicken:
*   1 whole chicken (about 3-4 lbs), cut into pieces, or 6-8 bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks
*   1/2 onion, quartered
*   2-3 cloves garlic, smashed
*   Salt to taste
*   Water or chicken broth

For the Mole Sauce:
*   Dried Chiles:
    *   4-5 Ancho chiles (for fruity depth and mild heat)
    *   3-4 Guajillo chiles (for fruity, tangy notes)
    *   2-3 Pasilla chiles (for earthy, smoky flavor)
    *   1 Mulato chile (optional, for deeper color and chocolatey notes)
*   Aromatics & Vegetables:
    *   1/2 small white onion, roughly chopped
    *   3-4 cloves garlic, peeled
    *   1 ripe tomato, roughly chopped (or 2-3 tomatillos)
*   Nuts & Seeds:
    *   1/4 cup blanched almonds
    *   1/4 cup raw peanuts
    *   2 tablespoons sesame seeds (plus more for garnish)
    *   1 tablespoon pumpkin seeds (pepitas)
*   Spices:
    *   1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds
    *   1/2 teaspoon coriander seeds
    *   4-5 whole cloves
    *   1/2 stick Mexican cinnamon (or 1/4 tsp ground cinnamon)
    *   3-4 black peppercorns
    *   Pinch of dried oregano
    *   Pinch of dried thyme
*   Thickeners & Other:
    *   1-2 stale corn tortillas, torn into pieces (or a slice of stale bread)
    *   1 tablespoon lard or vegetable oil
    *   1-2 ounces Mexican chocolate (like Ibarra or Abuelita), broken into pieces
    *   1-2 tablespoons piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) or dark brown sugar, to taste
    *   Salt to taste
    *   4-6 cups reserved chicken broth (from cooking the chicken)

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Pollo en Mole

Making Pollo en Mole is a multi-step process, but each stage builds on the last, leading to a truly rewarding culinary experience.

1.  **Prepare the Chicken:** Place the chicken pieces in a large pot with the quartered onion, smashed garlic, and a generous pinch of salt. Cover with water or chicken broth. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer until the chicken is tender and cooked through, about 30-40 minutes. Remove the chicken, reserving at least 4-6 cups of the cooking broth. Set chicken aside. Once cool enough to handle, you can remove the skin if desired.

2.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Remove stems and seeds from all dried chiles. Heat a dry skillet over medium-low heat and toast the chiles for 30-60 seconds per side until fragrant, being careful not to burn them. Place toasted chiles in a bowl and cover with hot water. Let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft and pliable.

3.  **Toast Nuts, Seeds, and Spices:** In the same dry skillet, one by one, toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds until lightly golden and fragrant. Remove and set aside. Then, toast the cumin seeds, coriander seeds, cloves, cinnamon, peppercorns, oregano, and thyme until aromatic. Be careful not to burn them.

4.  **Sauté Aromatics and Thickeners:** In a separate pan or the same skillet with a little oil, sauté the chopped onion, garlic, and tomato until softened. Add the torn corn tortillas (or bread) and fry briefly until lightly browned.

5.  **Blend the Mole Paste:** Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving a little of the soaking liquid. In a high-speed blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, toasted nuts and seeds, toasted spices, sautéed aromatics, and tortillas. Add about 2 cups of the reserved chicken broth. Blend until incredibly smooth. This may take several minutes, and you might need to add a little more broth or chile soaking liquid to achieve a thick, pourable paste. For an ultra-smooth sauce, you can pass the blended mixture through a fine-mesh sieve.

6.  **Cook the Mole Sauce:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, heat 1 tablespoon of lard or vegetable oil over medium heat. Pour in the blended mole paste. Be careful, as it will splatter. Cook the mole paste, stirring constantly, for at least 15-20 minutes. This step is crucial for developing the flavor and achieving the right texture. The paste will darken and thicken, and its raw chile flavor will transform.

7.  **Simmer and Finish:** Gradually stir in the remaining reserved chicken broth, about 1 cup at a time, until the sauce reaches your desired consistency (it should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon). Bring to a simmer, then add the Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (or brown sugar). Stir until the chocolate and sugar are completely melted and incorporated. Season generously with salt to taste. Continue to simmer the sauce over low heat for at least 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, to allow the flavors to meld and deepen. The longer it simmers, the richer it becomes.

8.  **Combine Chicken and Mole:** Add the cooked chicken pieces to the simmering mole sauce. Spoon the sauce over the chicken to coat. Continue to simmer for another 15-20 minutes, ensuring the chicken is thoroughly heated through and infused with the mole's flavors.

## Tips for the Perfect Chicken Mole Sauce

Creating a truly exceptional Chicken Mole Sauce involves attention to detail and a willingness to taste and adjust.

*   **Quality Chiles are Key:** Use fresh, pliable dried chiles. Avoid any that are brittle or dusty, as they will lack flavor.
*   **Don't Burn Ingredients:** When toasting chiles, nuts, and spices, keep the heat low to medium-low and watch them carefully. Burning will result in a bitter flavor.
*   **Blend Until Silky Smooth:** A powerful blender is your best friend. For the smoothest possible sauce, blend for a longer time than you think necessary, and consider straining it through a fine-mesh sieve to catch any stubborn bits of chile skin or spice.
*   **Patience in Simmering:** The initial frying of the mole paste and the final simmering are non-negotiable steps. These periods allow the complex flavors to deepen and meld, transforming the sauce from a collection of ingredients into a harmonious masterpiece.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Mole is all about balance. Continuously taste your sauce throughout the simmering process. Adjust salt, sweetness (with more piloncillo/sugar), or even a tiny splash of vinegar if you feel it needs more acidity.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole sauce often tastes even better the next day, after the flavors have had more time to marry. You can make the sauce a day or two in advance and gently reheat it before adding the chicken.

## Serving Suggestions for Chicken Mole

Pollo en Mole is a centerpiece dish, worthy of a special occasion or a comforting family meal. The traditional accompaniments are simple, allowing the rich sauce to shine. Serve your Chicken Mole generously over warm corn tortillas, allowing guests to make their own tacos. It’s also excellent alongside a bed of fluffy Mexican red rice or plain white rice, which helps to soak up every drop of that exquisite sauce. Garnish with a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, finely chopped white onion, or fresh cilantro for a pop of color and freshness. Pair it with a refreshing agua fresca, a cold Mexican lager, or a reposado tequila for a truly authentic dining experience.

## Variations and Substitutions for Mole Sauce

While Mole Poblano with chicken is iconic, the beauty of mole lies in its versatility. Don't be afraid to experiment:

*   **Proteins:** While chicken is traditional, mole pairs beautifully with other proteins. Try it with roasted turkey (especially for holidays), slow-cooked pork shoulder, beef short ribs, or even plant-based options like roasted sweet potatoes, cauliflower, or firm tofu for a vegetarian mole.
*   **Chiles:** Experiment with different chile combinations. For a spicier kick, add a few chiles de árbol or serrano peppers. For a smokier profile, a chipotle chile can be a wonderful addition.
*   **Nuts and Seeds:** Adjust the proportions of almonds, peanuts, and sesame seeds based on your preference. Walnuts or pecans can also be included for different nutty nuances.
*   **Chocolate:** If Mexican chocolate is unavailable, use unsweetened dark chocolate (70-85% cocoa solids) and adjust the piloncillo/sugar accordingly to balance the bitterness.
*   **Shortcuts:** For those truly pressed for time, high-quality pre-made mole pastes are available in Mexican markets or online. While they won't replicate the depth of a homemade sauce, they offer a delicious starting point. Just be sure to thin them with chicken broth and simmer them well.

Mastering the art of Pollo en Mole is a culinary journey that connects you to centuries of Mexican tradition. It's a dish that celebrates complexity, patience, and the incredible power of diverse ingredients uniting to create something truly magical. Don't let its lengthy ingredient list deter you; the process is meditative, and the final reward of a rich, flavorful Chicken Mole is an experience you won't soon forget. So gather your ingredients, clear your schedule, and prepare to embark on a delicious adventure into the heart of Mexican cuisine.
